text: The spotlight metaphor suggests attention is something we move. But neuroscientist Earl Miller's work on neural oscillations offers a complementary frame: attention is something we tune. Using multi-electrode recordings in macaque cortex, Miller's lab showed that selective attention increases gamma-band oscillations (30–80 Hz) in neurons coding the attended stimulus while increasing alpha-band oscillations (8–12 Hz) in neurons coding ignored stimuli. Alpha waves act as a cortical gate — high alpha means a neural population is effectively offline. Attention is thus less like pointing a flashlight and more like conducting an orchestra: selectively raising and lowering the amplitude of competing neural ensembles.
question: According to Earl Miller's research, what is the role of alpha-band oscillations in selective attention?

options.text: They enhance signal transmission to the attended stimulus.
They synchronize prefrontal and parietal regions to coordinate the spotlight.
They gate ignored neurons offline, suppressing unattended stimuli.
They increase metabolic activity in the locus coeruleus during focus.
